INTELLIGENCE BRIEF: Gene Therapy for Spinal Muscular Atrophy Type 2 — ZolgenMax

PROGRAM OVERVIEW
ZolgenMax is an intrathecal AAV9-based gene therapy delivering a functional copy of the SMN1 gene to motor neurons in SMA Type 2 patients aged 2-12 years. Unlike Zolgensma (IV, <2 years old only), ZolgenMax targets the underserved older pediatric population.

CLINICAL DATA
- Phase II SMART-2 Trial (N=42): 71% of patients achieved ≥3-point improvement on HFMSE
- Motor function: Mean HFMSE increase of 5.2 points at 12 months (clinically meaningful threshold: 3 points)
- Durability: 18-month data shows sustained benefit without decline in 89% of responders
- Safety: 2 cases of transient transaminitis (resolved with corticosteroids); 1 serious dorsal root ganglion toxicity requiring hospitalization
- No treatment-related deaths

COMPETITIVE LANDSCAPE
- Spinraza (nusinersen): Established, chronic intrathecal dosing, well-characterized safety profile
- Evrysdi (risdiplam): Oral, convenient, growing market share especially in older patients
- Zolgensma: IV gene therapy, limited to <2 years, one-time dose
- Key differentiator: One-time treatment for the 2-12 age gap not covered by Zolgensma

MARKET CONSIDERATIONS
- Ultra-rare: ~3,000-5,000 SMA Type 2 patients in US
- Pricing benchmark: Zolgensma at $2.1M; market expects $1.5-2.5M for ZolgenMax
- Payer resistance: Outcomes-based contracts likely required for reimbursement
- Patient identification: Many patients already on Spinraza/Evrysdi — switching dynamics complex
- KOL sentiment: Cautiously optimistic; want longer durability and DRG toxicity data
- Parent advocacy: SMA community highly organized and vocal; strong pull-through potential

DECISION QUESTIONS
1. What is the probability of successful Phase III completion and FDA approval by 2027?
2. Will payers cover at the expected price point without outcomes-based contracts?
3. Can ZolgenMax capture patients currently stable on Spinraza or Evrysdi?
